extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a universal rule for all deceased members of the community.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to reciprocal gift-giving and entertainment
proof: [§307] For if thou hadst found him yet alive in the land of Ithaca, then would he have sent thee on thy way with ample requital of gifts and good entertainment; for that is the due of him who begins the kindness.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes that a guest who has initiated hospitality is legally or customarily entitled to 'ample requital of gifts' (antidōra) and entertainment from the host. This status-based right (the 'due') governs interactions between travel-bound foreigners and local elites.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While common, this specifically governs the interaction of elites and recognized guests from other lands.

proof: [§310] But they themselves went together to the place of assembly, sad at heart... For a shame is this even for men that are yet to be to hear of, if we shall not take vengeance on the slayers of our sons and our brothers.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The grieving families of the suitors gather in a formal assembly (agora) to debate and organize a collective military strike for 'venezance.' This indicates that citizens have the standing to use the assembly to mobilize for blood-justice.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to the citizen body of Ithaca capable of attending the agora.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to address the public assembly
proof: [§311] Then among them spoke the old lord Halitherses, son of Mastor, for he alone saw before and after: he with good intent addressed their assembly, and said: “Hearken now to me, men of Ithaca, to the word that I shall say."
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text depicts a formal assembly where an elder (Halitherses) has the recognized right to address the gathered 'men of Ithaca' to influence collective decision-making, reflecting a standard political right for male citizens of standing.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While any man in the assembly might listen, the right to formal address is generally reserved for heads of households or 'lords' within the citizen body.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to bear arms in defense of the household
proof: [§312] Odysseus and his men were four, and six the sons of Dolius, and among them Laertes and Dolius donned their armour, grey-headed though they were, warriors perforce.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Despite their advanced age ('grey-headed'), the text shows that elderly men retain the right and obligation to arm themselves and participate in combat when the household is threatened, indicating that age does not legally or customarily bar them from military action.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all elderly free males in a state of conflict or household defense.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to lifelong rule and amnesty
proof: [§311] Now that goodly Odysseus has taken vengeance on the wooers, let them swear a solemn oath, and let him be king all his days, and let us on our part bring about a forgetting of the slaying of their sons and brothers; and let them love one another as before, and let wealth and peace abound.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a rule for political stability where the king's right to rule for life is affirmed in exchange for a legal/religious 'forgetting' (amnesty) of past violence, showing the king's central role in the legal order.
